title: Nonperforming Loan Disposals Without Resolution
abstract: Using proprietary data on nonperforming loan (NPL) transactions in China, we document that banks conceal NPLs in disposal transactions that are supposed to resolve NPLs. In these transactions, banks provide financing and retain the credit risks and debt collection obligations. Transaction prices contradict underlying credit risks: Despite being delinquent for 4.5 years, the median NPL package is sold at face value with zero haircut. The majority of NPL packages are resold at a premium to banks’ non–government-owned borrowers, further supporting the concealment view. Banks under stricter regulation conceal NPLs more and engage in more illicit concealment. NPL-concealing banks increase zombie lending, suggesting that an ineffective resolution exacerbates credit misallocation. Recognizing the hidden NPLs implies the total NPLs in China is two to three times the reported amount.

author.Ben Charoenwong: National University of Singapore Business School, Singapore 119245, Singapore;
author.Meng Miao: Renmin University of China School of Finance, Beijing 100872, China
author.Tianyue Ruan: National University of Singapore Business School, Singapore 119245, Singapore;

article.1. Introduction: Nonperforming loans (NPLs) weaken bank health, curtail bank credit supply, and can lead to elevated risks of systemic financial crises and real economic disruptions (Bernanke et al. 1991,Calomiris and Mason 2003,Iyer et al. 2014,Iacoviello 2015). A common method of NPL resolution is to create designated financial institutions—asset management companies (AMCs)—to purchase and resolve banks’ NPLs. Also known as the “bad bank” model, this method for NPL resolution has been viewed by policymakers as a viable method to resolve distressed banking assets (Geithner 2009,Avgouleas and Goodhart 2017).1Despite their wide use, whether AMCs achieve orderly resolution of NPLs is theoretically ambiguous: On the one hand, AMCs may have expertise and economies of scale in NPL resolution to avoid fire sales and help restore investors’ confidence in troubled banks. On the other hand, AMCs are specially chartered institutions that enjoy exclusive access to purchase NPLs, typically in opaque transactions whose details are not publicly disclosed or known to regulators. As a result, AMCs may be prone to capture by banks who are incentivized to avoid orderly resolution, which typically forces the bank to recognize substantial asset losses and often triggers a downward revaluation. Whether NPL transactions are legitimate orderly resolution or illegitimate concealment of NPLs is important for financial stability. Resolution of NPLs would make banks safer, whereas concealment of NPLs obscures the risks in the financial system. However, most uses of AMCs to resolve NPLs in the financial sector do not strictly monitor how an AMC transacts with banks; thus, this imperfectly targeted government support can make banks’ actions strategically complementary (Farhi and Tirole 2012). If a bank finds out that its competitors are concealing NPLs, it is also more willing to conceal NPLs.2The key to distinguishing whether the AMC model is effective in resolving NPLs in the financial system is the nature of risk transfer. In an orderly resolution, all NPL risks are transferred from the banks to the AMC and systemic risk is reduced. In concealment, banks remain exposed to the transferred NPLs’ risks and systemic risk may be unchanged or even exacerbated if banks then increase risky lending. The opportunity to conceal NPLs may also exacerbate credit misallocation, because banks with the means to conceal NPLs are less concerned with loan quality and may be more willing to extend credit to unworthy distressed borrowers. Using detailed data on NPL transactions in China, we study whether NPL transactions appear to be orderly resolutions and whether financial regulation stringency affects banks’ NPL offloading behavior. Chinese banks have increasingly offloaded NPLs in recent years. In 2019, they disposed of 2.3 trillion yuan (more than US$300 billion) of nonperforming assets, whereas the end-of-year reported NPLs amounted to 2.41 trillion yuan.3AMCs, the designated debt resolution specialists, are granted exclusive rights to purchase NPLs in bundles from banks. We source detailed NPL transaction-level data from a large AMC, with a sample spanning from its establishment in 2014 to 2019. During this period, the AMC conducted transactions worth over US$23 billion from all the Big 4 state-owned banks, 8 of the 12 joint-stock banks, and 70 smaller regional banks. We first document several features of the NPL transactions that cast doubt on the orderly resolution hypothesis but are consistent with banks concealing NPLs with cooperation from AMCs. First, more than 90% of NPL purchases are financed by the banks offloading the NPLs. Second, in all transactions, the NPL risks are transferred back to the banks using a return swap agreement, enabling the AMC to bear no credit risks at all. Third, all transaction contracts feature a clause whereby the AMC delegates the obligation to collect the NPLs back to the banks. Several stylized facts further bolster the view that the NPLs are concealed rather than resolved. Despite an average delinquency of 4.5 years, the mean NPL package sales haircut relative to face value is 5.1% and median is zero—imposing no write-offs or loss recognition for the offloading banks. Transaction prices do not correlate with the length of delinquency or other measures of NPL quality. In addition, the AMC resells more than 95% of the NPL packages with no securitization or rebundling at strictly positive resale premiums that range between 0.15% and 3% after two years on average. The AMC holds NPL packages for exactly 6, 12, 18, 24, or 36 months before the resales, suggesting that the resale is prearranged. Resale premiums are proportional only to the AMC’s holding period, consistent with a fees paid to AMC for faciliating the concealment. Among the resold NPL packages, more than 80% are resold to third parties who are non–government-owned borrowers of the NPL-originating banks, and more than 85% of the third parties are in the manufacturing, accommodation, food, or wholesale and retail industries. We then study the impact of financial regulation on banks’ NPL concealment activity. In China, banks are subject to a formula-based regulatory minimum on the allowance for loan impairment losses relative to NPLs. Offloading NPLs to AMCs helps to satisfy financial requirements and benefits banks under pressure to comply with regulations. Consistent with the regulatory pressure, we find NPL transactions concentrate in the week immediately before quarterly regulatory reporting. In addition, we find that banks in violation of the 150% minimum allowance-to-NPLs ratio are 25 percentage points more likely to offload NPLs to the AMC in the subsequent year. Enforcement is also important: those violators facing stricter regulators are more likely to conceal NPLs to avoid regulatory violations. NPL concealment seems effective, as banks are less likely to violate the 150% minimum allowance-to-NPLs ratio in the subsequent year after NPL concealment. These results suggest that regulation arbitrage constitutes one incentive of NPL concealment. Whether banks are in violation of the NPL threshold also affects the financing method for NPL concealment. For originating banks, lending directly to the AMC to finance an NPL transaction is more easily detected and therefore riskier than indirect financing through a trust company. We find that banks with lower capitalization or those that violated the 150% minimum allowance-to-NPL ratio in the previous year are more likely to finance the NPL transactions through a direct loan, consistent with them being more desperate to conceal NPLs. In July 2019, the China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC) issued a ban on the direct lending of banks to local AMCs to finance NPL transactions, revealing that the regulators are aware of the illicit concealment activity to some extent. Following the rule, we find that banks switch to indirect financing. Last, we examine the effect of NPL concealment on banks’ credit allocation. On the one hand, transferring NPLs to AMCs cosmetically improves their balance sheet health and allows banks to increase lending to profitable opportunities. On the other hand, the possibility to conceal NPLs mitigates banks’ concern with loan quality regulation or future losses triggered by asset write-downs and may encourage them to extend credit to unworthy distressed borrowers, a phenomenon coined as “zombie lending.” Applying financial-data-based measures of zombie firms (Altman et al. 2021,Acharya et al. 2024) to banks’ borrowers, we show that NPL-concealing banks increase their zombie credit by 350%, suggesting that an ineffective resolution substantially exacerbates credit misallocation. Although the opaqueness of the market and the illicit nature of hiding NPLs make it challenging to precisely evaluate the external validity of our findings, we believe that our findings are broadly representative for four reasons. First, with regard to the representativeness of the AMC, we find the local AMC has characteristics similar to the average local AMC in terms of leverage ratio and size. In addition, local AMCs are set up following low gross domestic product (GDP) growth, and the province where this AMC is headquartered in is not the worst nor best province in terms of GDP growth. Therefore, we believe that if the incentives for local AMCs to conceal NPLs exist in one province, it is probable that they will exist in other provinces as well. Second, with regard to the representativeness of the NPL transactions, our data from the AMC include 82 banks from all 31 municipalities and provinces in mainland China. The wide coverage of this AMC’s counter-party banks and empirical analyses controlling for unobserved bank heterogeneity suggest that bank-specific idiosyncratic characteristics do not drive our results. In addition, the relationships between the AMC and large national and joint-stock banks are mostly with the local branches in the AMC’s province. Third, banking regulators’ actions against concealment activity suggest that the problem is widespread and serious. Apart from the aforementioned ban on direct lending to finance NPL transactions, at least nine municipalities and provinces in China launched investigations and changed regulatory practices to clamp down on the pass-through business of local AMCs by the end of 2020.4 Last, several local AMCs have been reported in the Chinese financial press to coordinate with banks and act as pass-through entities using structures in line with our findings.5In June 2022, China’s National Audit Office found that 23 small- and medium-sized banks underdisclosed nonperforming assets of 171 billion yuan.6further corroborating our findings. The multitude of empirical evidence lends credence to the possibility that hidden NPLs are prevalent. Overall, our empirical evidence of NPL transactions in China also highlights a key friction that exists in many countries’ implementation of the bad bank model: Imperfectly targeted government support induces complementarities for banks’ actions (Farhi and Tirole 2012). Combined with binding financial regulations, the bad bank model with little oversight on NPL transactions can incentivize banks to devise transactions to hide their troubled assets (Thomson 2011). By documenting the coordination between banks and AMCs to conceal NPLs, we also shed light on the policy design of problem loan resolution. As stated inEuropean Systemic Risk Board (2018, p. 23), “macroprudential authorities should be able to monitor credit and other developments in a timely manner from a system-wide perspective, in order to identify…the risks that are building up at [the] banking system level and which may end up causing a system-wide increase in NPLs.” Furthermore, NPL-concealing banks extend more zombie credit, suggesting that an ineffective resolution exacerbates credit misallocation. Because banks remain exposed to the NPLs that were sold, they represent hidden NPLs that should be recognized in calculating total NPLs. To study how potential realized losses may affect the financial system, we conduct a back-of-the-envelope stress test analysis assuming that our findings are representative of local AMCs and excluding national AMCs that are financial conglomerates with various other businesses. We find that hidden NPLs could amount to 2.81 to 4.62 trillion RMB in 2019 (between 117% and 192% higher than the official statistics), implying a total NPL ratio between 3.87% and 5.14% compared with the official reported NPL ratio of 1.86%. Our study contributes to the literature on the measurement and resolution of NPLs. Bank distresses and failures are characterized by a high fraction of NPLs and poor asset quality (Demirgüç-Kunt 1989,Cole and White 2012,DeYoung and Torna 2013). Unresolved NPLs on banks’ balance sheets curtail potential credit supply. As borrowers and lenders form relationships, borrowers cannot easily substitute for lost bank financing, leading to real economic disruptions.7Jiménez et al. (2017)argue that high NPLs impede bank responses to countercyclical capital buffers due to binding market constraints. Therefore, accurately and quickly recognizing NPLs is crucial for assessing the safety of banks and the stability of the financial system and economy as a whole. Our study is also related to the literature on the effectiveness of financial regulation. Financial institutions are widely documented to take on risks and financial structures to circumvent the significant regulatory requirements and monitoring requirements imposed on them.Acharya et al. (2013)document that commercial banks securitize assets without risk transfers and yet reduce regulatory capital requirement prior to the Global Financial Crisis.Begley et al. (2017)find that banks strategically under-report the risk in their trading books to avoid capital surcharge.Flanagan and Purnanandam (2019)study a regulation change in India that revealed banks underreported their losses. At a broader level, banking regulation may lead to unintended consequences on other aspects of the financial system.Nadauld and Sherlund (2013)show that a 2004 change in the capital requirements for investment banks spurred them to engage in excessive securitization. In terms of banks’ “regulatory arbitrage” actions,Acharya et al. (2019)document that wealth management products typically mature at quarter ends, helping the issuing banks to temporarily boost the level of deposits to stay below the loan-deposit limit, consistent with the seasonality in NPL sales at the end of quarters which we observe. Along a similar vein, our findings contribute to the literature by documenting an alternative means through which banks may evade regulatory scrutiny, with systemic risk implications.
article.2. Institutional Setting and Data: 2.1. Management of Nonperforming Loans in China The China Banking Regulatory Commission (CBRC) and its successor, the China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC),8regulate banks and their asset quality. Since 1998, commercial banks in China are required to adopt a five-category risk-based classification for their loans. The five categories, in increasing order of riskiness, are “normal,” “special mention,” “substandard,” “doubtful,” and “loss.” The last three categories are collectively considered NPLs. Although classifying loans over 90 days past due as NPLs is a commonly adopted standard internationally,9there is no direct mapping between the delinquency status and the five-category classification in China. Instead, banks are allowed to use their internal risk models to classify the loans in the five-category system. Following the main recommendations from China’s 2017 Financial Sector Assessment Program (FSAP) and the Basel Committee’s final guidance on thePrudential treatment of problem assets: Definitions of nonperforming exposures and forbearance, the CBIRC made theProvisional Measures for the Risk Classification of Commercial Bank Financial Assets (Public Comment Draft)available to the public for comment on April 30, 2019. TheProvisional Measures (Public Comment Draft)requires that loans over 90 days past due (including after rollovers) shall be classified as nonperforming, even when collateral is assessed to be sufficient. As of this writing, the final ruling on the provisional measures has not been established. AMCs were first created by the central government between 1998 to 1999 to clean up the growing NPLs of state-owned commercial banks, which rose to 25% of total loans in the Asian Financial Crisis (Chen 2002,Fung and Ma 2002). A dedicated AMC was set up for each of the Big 4 state-owned banks to acquire and then resolve its NPLs. Similar to AMCs or “bad banks” in other countries such as the Resolution Trust Corporation in the United States (FDIC 1997,1998), the AMCs are the most likely savior of the banking system (IMF 2012). Without such specialized vehicles to absorb toxic assets, banks are forced to hold onto NPLs and may lack resources to make new loans (Fredriksson and Frykström 2019). A total of 1.3 trillion RMB of NPLs made before 1996 were transferred from banks to the AMCs at their face value (Peiser and Wang 2002). In the 2000s, the four national AMCs participated in several subsequent waves of NPL transfer to improve the commercial banks’ asset quality for their IPOs (Pomerleano and Shaw 2005,Luo 2016). Following the AMC model used in the past, these national AMCs were initially established in 1999 with a tenure of 10 years. Late in 2009, their tenure was extended to an indefinite period. In addition, the restrictions placed upon the national AMCs to purchase NPLs from their specific affiliated banks were also lifted, so from then on, they were free to purchase NPLs from any financial institution. In recent years, the four national AMCs have become financial conglomerates with various other business lines. After the Global Financial Crisis, the Chinese government adopted multiple initiatives to reduce leverage and combat distressed debt more specifically, including the permission to establish local AMCs. In 2012, the Ministry of Finance of China (MOF) and the CBRC jointly issued theMeasures for the Administration of the Batch Transfer of Non-Performing Assets of Financial Enterprisesto permit the establishment of local AMCs to acquire NPLs from banks. Initially, only one local AMC could be established per province. Furthermore, local AMCs were not allowed to sell the NPLs they acquired to other entities, and the only resolution permitted was debt restructuring. In 2016, a regulatory amendment relaxed the entry restriction to two local AMCs per province and allowed local AMCs to sell their inventory of NPLs to entities in other provinces (CBRC 2016). The local AMCs are directly monitored and regulated by the local governments and are subject to the CBIRC certification for their distressed debt business. National AMCs, on the other hand, are deemed as nondepository banking institutions and are therefore regulated by the CBIRC. By the end of 2019, 59 local AMCs were set up and certified by the CBIRC. All 31 provinces in mainland China had at least one local AMC, and most provinces had two.10Banks transfer NPLs to AMCs in the form of NPL packages consisting of at least 10 NPLs (also called a “batch” or “bulk” transfer). In such a transaction, the risks associated with the NPLs are deemed as being transferred to the AMC, and therefore the bank removes the transferred NPLs from its balance sheet. Under the current regulatory framework, only designated national and local AMCs can receive bulk NPL transfers from banks.11In other words, transferring to AMCs is the only viable option for banks to dispose NPLs in bulk. 2.2. Data Sources We combine proprietary and publicly available data from several different data sources for our analyses. First, we obtain information on all NPL transactions from a leading local AMC in a relatively developed province of China. For security reasons, we mask the company. This local AMC has similar size and leverage as an average local AMC. The sample of NPL transactions spans from the AMC’s establishment in the third quarter of 2014 through the end of 2019. As our data comes from one AMC, the Online Appendix, Section A.4, further assesses the representativeness of our AMC with respect to the whole market using AMCs’ financial data. Then, we supplement the proprietary data with bank-level financial information and other characteristics from Wind and BankScope. We include measures of local economic conditions, such as local GDP growth, local tax revenue growth, and local unemployment rate, as control variables in our regression analyses. As inChen et al. (2019), we also include the percentage change in electricity use as an alternative measure of local economic conditions, which may be less susceptible to manipulation. Data on economic indicators are obtained from the National Bureau of Statistics of China, OECD, the World Bank, and other publicly available sources through the Wind and CEIC data aggregators. Whenever the same variables are available in multiple databases, we cross-check the data to ensure consistency and accuracy. Our sample covers 257 NPL transactions with a total amount traded of 165 billion RMB (approx. US$23 billion), consisting of a total of 29,555 loans made to 22,230 borrowers by 82 banks from all 31 municipalities and provinces in mainland China. The sample includes all Big 4 state-owned commercial banks, 8 joint-stock banks, and 70 smaller regional banks. In addition, 7 of the banks in our sample are headquartered in first-tier cities, 12 banks in second-tier cities, and 62 banks in third-tier cities.Table 1reports the characteristics of banks covered in our sample. The median total loans is 51 billion RMB (approximately US$7.3 billion), and the median profits are 1.36 billion RMB (approximately US$194 million). The median bank-year observation satisfies the bank capital regulation with a 13% capital ratio, and the minimum capital ratio is 10.8%, slightly above the 10.5% regulatory minimum. Table 1.Bank Characteristics Table 1.Bank Characteristics Notes.Summary statistics at the bank-year level showing both bank characteristics in Panel A and local economic conditions in Panel B. The panel contains 82 banks and 409 bank-year observations. Where a bank has more than one NPL transaction in a year, we sum the size of NPL transactions and value-weight by face value to get the average NPL delinquency in months. Local economic conditions refer to province characteristics in a bank’ headquarter province. bn, billions; mn, millions; dec, decimal. All level figures are reported in Chinese Yuan (RMB). Apart from counts, which are shown as integers, all figures are rounded to either three significant digits or three decimal places, whichever is shorter. In our data, large state-owned banks, joint stock banks, and smaller regional banks accounted for 38%, 19%, and 43% of total banking assets, respectively. As a comparison, they accounted for 40%, 17%, and 43% of the NPL transaction size in our data. The Online Appendix, Section A.2, further analyzes the sample selection based on observable characteristics and finds that the most important predictor for sample entry is the distance from a bank to the AMC’s headquarter. The AMC’s transactions with national and larger regional banks occur through local branches in the same province of the AMC. The local nature of the transactions may either reflect a local information edge or close coordination between banks and the AMC in the NPL transactions. We discuss these hypotheses and further empirical evidence in the next section.
article.3. Are NPL Transactions Resolution or Co: Whether NPL transactions from banks to AMCs can represent orderly resolution or concealment of NPLs is important for financial stability. Resolution of NPLs would make banks safer, whereas concealment of NPLs obscures the risks in the financial system. The key to tell the two apart is the nature of risk transfer. In orderly resolution, all NPL risks are completely transferred from the banks to the AMC, consistent with the accounting practice of removing the transferred NPLs from bank balance sheets. In concealment, banks remain exposed to the transferred NPLs’ risks. In this section, we examine the NPL transaction characteristics and outcomes to analyze the nature of risk transfer in the data. 3.1. Characteristics of NPL Transactions Table 2presents the characteristics of the 257 NPL transactions in our sample. Panel A reports the composition of NPLs in the packages. The mean and median size of the transacted NPL packages are 642 million and 264 million, respectively. The average transacted NPL package includes 108 loans from 82 borrowers and has an average delinquency of 55 months. Around 85% of the loans are corporate loans with contractual maturity of less than one year. Therefore, although a typical loan in the transacted package matures in less than one year, the borrower has not repaid the loan for more than 4.4 years. Around one third of the loans are collateralized and the rest are either guaranteed or unsecured.12We discuss additional NPL characteristics and also document patterns of NPL transactions over time in the Online Appendix, Section A.3. Table 2.NPL Transaction Details Table 2.NPL Transaction Details Notes.Summary statistics at the transaction level, showing information for NPL transactions and package characteristics. Observations are at the NPL transaction level, and we observe 257 transactions. bn, billions in Chinese Yuan (RMB); dec, decimal. Examining contract features, we find that all transactions contain contract features that are inconsistent with orderly resolution. First, all transactions have a clause detailing the financing provided to the AMC to purchase the NPLs. The exact form of financing can be traced to the banks for more than 90% of NPL transactions (Section 4.3). Second, all transactions have a “return swap” clause where the AMC swaps the returns of the NPLs with the banks and receive the returns of the banks’ loans to itself. This clause means that the AMC does not bear the credit risks of the NPLs. Third, all transactions include a collection clause that delegates NPL collection obligations back to the initiating banks. Furthermore, 96.5% of contracts contain an appendix stating that after the contract expires, the contract will renew, or another AMC or third party will purchase the NPL package. Importantly, all transaction contracts also include a confidentiality clause whereby all contents of the contract are deemed strictly confidential by the two signing parties (including in front of the court) unless “necessary.”13Finally, all the contract features are written as separate contracts. Anecdotally, the separation of the contract terms into different contracts facilitates selective reporting to the regulator. For instance, a bank or the AMC can present only the main contract concerning the NPL transaction, which does not contain any of these clauses, to the regulator if they are inspected. Next, we turn to the pricing of the NPL packages. 3.2. Pricing of NPL Transactions The haircut of the NPL transaction price relative to the face value of the loans has a mean of 5.1% and a median of 0%.Figure 1shows the empirical cumulative distribution of haircut. Despite an average delinquency of almost 4.5 years, 60% of all NPLs were sold at exactly zero haircuts; 30% of NPLs were sold at a positive haircut but only less than 3% were sold at a haircut of 30% or more; 8% of NPL packages were sold even at a premium relative to face value. The observed haircuts are in stark contrast to the typical haircuts in sales of nonperforming assets.14 We assess the relation between haircut and the characteristics of sold NPLs using the following panel regression specification: Table 3presents the results for four different measures of characteristics of sold NPLs: the average delinquency, the share of loans to state-owned enterprises (SOEs), the share of secured loans, and the share of loans with maturity greater than one year. We find NPL packages with a one-standard-deviation higher average delinquency of 14 months carry a lower haircut of 2.8 percentage points (approximately 28% of one standard deviation), whereas none of the other three measures that positively capture NPL quality are correlated with NPL pricing. These findings contradict the notion that transaction prices reflect the underlying NPL risks. In addition, we find NPL transactions from banks with higher capital ratios have higher haircuts, although the economic significance is not large. A one-standard-deviation higher capital ratio of 1.6 percentage points corresponds with a lower haircut of around 0.5% relative to face value or less than 6% of a standard deviation of haircuts. Nonetheless, for an orderly resolution, we would have expected banks with lower capital ratios, unhealthier banks, to sell at lower prices if they were more eager to offload NPLs. Table 3.Pricing of NPL Transactions Table 3.Pricing of NPL Transactions Notes.The table shows the relation between NPL package characteristics and prices. Observations are at the bank-year level. All regressions include controls for bank health and local economic conditions, including the lagged deposit growth, the lagged loan growth, the lagged reported GDP growth in the banks’ headquarter province, and the lagged growth in electricity usage in the banks’ headquarter province, as well as year and bank fixed effects.tstatistics testing the coefficients with zero are shown in parentheses, where standard errors are clustered by bank. ***, **, and *Statistical significance at the 1% level, 5% level, and 10% level, respectively. 3.3. Prevalence of NPL Resales The elevated prices we observe in NPL transactions raise a puzzle: Why would the AMC ever pay more than the face value for NPLs? A possible explanation is that the AMC expects to make a profit through its handling of the loans. We next analyze how the AMC handles the NPL packages after purchasing them from the banks. Table 4, Panel A, traces out the decomposition of the AMC’s handling of NPL packages conditional on the time elapsed since the initial NPL transactions. Within the first year after the initial NPL purchases from banks, the AMC keeps 67.7% of NPL packages while selling 2.94% of the packages to another AMC and 27.4% to a different entity, which we call a third-party. Resales account for almost one-third of the NPL packages within one year and increase to the vast majority in about two to three years. After two years, the fraction of NPL packages staying with the AMC further drops to 3% or below, and the fraction of resold packages is greater than 96%. Third parties are the major buyers of the NPL packages in the resales. Overall, reselling the NPL packages is the dominant approach that the AMC adopts to handle the NPL packages it purchases from banks, and it on average takes the AMC about two to three years to complete the resales. Table 4.Ultimate Owners and Resale Transactions Table 4.Ultimate Owners and Resale Transactions Notes.Summary statistics at the transaction level, showing information for NPL transactions and package characteristics. Observations are at the NPL transaction level. In Panel A, the probability is expressed in percentage points (%) and rounded to two decimal places. Percentages may not sum up to 100% due to rounding. Panel B shows that all the resold NPLs are sold at a premium relative to AMC’s purchase price: The minimum premium is 0.15%, and the maximum is 3%, with a mean of 1% and a median of 0.9%. Even among the NPLs purchased at a premium relative to face value, we find an average of 0.6% resale premium. None of the resold NPL packages undergoes another round of bundling or repackaging (“re-syndication”). All of them are resold “as is” to either another AMC or a third-party buyer. The majority of resold NPL packages are purchased by third-party buyers; therefore, we focus on their characteristics next. Although we are unable to reveal exact identities of third-party buyers due to confidentiality reasons, we provide information on their characteristics.15For all the NPL packages resold to a third-party buyer, the third-party buyer is located in the same city as the exact bank that originates the NPLs in the package. In addition, none of the third parties are SOEs. In terms of the industry of third parties, the modal industry is in manufacturing, followed by accommodation, and then wholesale and retail industries. These three industries comprise more than 85% of the third-party buyers. To the extent that some NPLs were collateralized by land or real estate, we expect third parties to be real estate companies. Yet, only a little over 2% of the third parties are in the real estate industry. Therefore, the third parties that become the owners of the NPL packages after the resales do not seem to be debt resolution specialists. Last but not least, the majority of the third parties are affiliates of the original banks as measured at the bank-NPL package level: 95% of the third parties are existing borrowers of the banks who offloaded the packages, and an additional 3% are discounted bill users of the banks. Only 1.4% of the third parties do not have a prior borrowing relationship with the banks and therefore are classified as “others.” In other words, the resales of NPL packages are highly targeted: an NPL package initially sold by a bank is resold as is to the bank’s affiliate.16 We calculate the AMC’s holding period using the transaction dates of initial and resale transactions and examine its relationship with the resale premium. The scatter plot of the AMC’s holding period and the resale premium (Figure 2) shows that for an NPL package which is eventually resold, the AMC holds the package for either exactly 6, 12, 18, 24, or 36 months. The resale premium is a step function of the length of the AMC’s holding period—the longer the holding period, the higher the resale premium.17Observing the precise transaction dates (as opposed to transaction months) allows us to detect a holding period that is not mechanically rounded. Nonetheless, the AMC’s holding period exhibits exact clustering in multiples of 6 months in the data, which is inconsistent with the alternative interpretation of patient resolution. If NPLs were sold to the AMC with no prearranged resales, and the AMC considers reselling the NPLs through auctions or other markets, there should be no obvious clustering in the AMC’s holding periods. However, if the resale transactions were part of the coordination between banks and AMCs, the AMC’s holding period is likely to be agreed upon ex ante, where NPL intermediation periods in multiples of six months may emerge. Therefore, the positive relationship between the AMC holding period and the resale premium likely reflects compensation for the AMC to temporarily hold on to NPLs. The AMC charges a higher intermediation fee for holding on to an NPL package longer with its balance sheet capacity. Overall, the analysis on the resale prices and counter-parties shows that the AMC passes through the NPL packages from banks to third parties who are close affiliates of the originating banks. The banks appear to proportionally compensate the AMC for how long it holds on to NPL packages. 3.4. How Do Banks Conceal NPLs? Although the stylized facts do not prove that all NPL transactions reflect concealment, they suggest that NPL transactions most likely do not represent true risk transfers of NPLs on average. First, more than 90% of NPL purchases are financed by the banks offloading the NPLs. Second, in all transactions, the NPL risks are transferred back to the banks using a return swap agreement, enabling the AMC to bear no credit risks at all. Third, despite that AMCs are supposed to be debt resolution specialists, all transaction contracts feature a clause whereby the AMC delegates the obligation to collect the NPLs back to the banks. Several other stylized facts further bolster the view that the NPLs are concealed rather than resolved. Despite an average delinquency of 4.5 years, NPL packages are sold at elevated prices. The mean and median haircuts relative to the face value are 5.1% and 0%, respectively, and more than 60% of the NPLs are traded with exactly 0% haircut. Transaction prices also do not correlate with measures of NPL quality such as the length of delinquency. In addition, the AMC resells more than 95% of the NPL packages with no securitization or rebundling at strictly positive resale premiums that range between 0.15% and 3% after two years on average. The AMC holds NPL packages for exactly 6, 12, 18, 24, or 36 months before the resales, suggesting that the resale is prearranged. Resale premiums are proportional only to the AMC’s holding period, indicating they represent fixed fees paid to AMC. Among the resold NPL packages, more than 80% are resold to third parties who are non–government-owned borrowers of the NPL-originating banks, and more than 85% of the third parties are in the manufacturing, accommodation, food, or wholesale and retail industries. Given the lack of risk transfers and the passive role of the AMC in the process, actual resolution of the NPL risks is highly unlikely. Instead, these stylized patterns are consistent with banks concealing NPLs with cooperation from the AMC and remaining exposed to the NPL risks. In the Online Appendix, Section A.6, we address several additional alternative explanations, including government-backed resolution and strategic hold-out of NPLs due to information advantages. Our analyses reveal three entities are involved in the concealment process asFigure 3shows18: (1) bank A provides funds to the AMC to purchase NPL package from itself, (2) the AMC buys the NPL package typically at the face value and holds it temporarily, while being compensated for acting as a pass-through entity, and (3) after six months to three years, one of bank A’s existing borrowers buys the NPL package as-is from the AMC at a premium. Participating in the sale and resale process generates a sizable profit for an AMC. For an average NPL package of 438 million RMB (approximately US$62.6 million), a median 0.9% resale premium is 3.94 million RMB (approximately US$604,000) for holding the NPLs on its book temporarily before reselling it to the bank’s third-party affiliates. Across all transactions from 2014 to 2019, the AMC would have earned more than 1 billion RMB (approximately US$150 million) in revenues for participating as a pass-through entity. These revenues are likely larger than the risky recovery on NPLs that are more than 40 months delinquent. The coordination between banks and the AMC is also self-enforcing. As a bank, the exposure of the contracts would expose not only their conduct of NPL concealment but also their high NPL rate. As an AMC, the exposure of contracts means that their reputation among banks would be damaged, jeopardizing other transactions of the same kind. Finally, anecdotal evidence suggests that the bank has a similar financial arrangement with the third party as they do with the AMCs: They provide funding, and they use the return swap clause and the collection obligation delegation clause to ensure that the third party takes no risk and no responsibility to collect NPLs and they only charge fixed rate fees. NPL packages are offloaded first to AMCs because they have exclusive rights to purchase NPL packages in large quantities.
article.4. Bank Regulation and NPL Concealment: In this section, we test the impact of financial regulation on banks’ NPL concealment activity. We first describe the quantity-based loan quality regulation in China and then show empirical evidence that higher regulatory pressure predicts more NPL concealment. 4.1. Regulatory Setting Banks are required to report three key ratios of loan quality in China: (1) the NPL ratio, defined as the balance of NPLs relative to the total balance of loans and advances to customers; (2) the allowance-to-NPLs ratio, defined as the allowance for loan impairment losses relative to the total balance of NPLs; and (3) the allowance-to-total loans ratio, defined as the allowance for loan impairment losses relative to the total balance of loans and advances to customers. Banks disclose the balance of NPLs and these three ratios in their quarterly, semiannual, and annual financial reports. Since 2011, the two allowance ratios have regulatory minimums and stipulate how banks set aside allowance for loan losses. More NPLs force banks to set aside higher allowances that reduces their profits. This quantity-based loan quality regulation approach differs from the mandatory charge-off of delinquent loans in other countries.19Chinese banks are not required to charge off delinquent loans.20Originally, the regulatory minimums for the allowance-to-NPLs ratio and the allowance-to-total loans ratio were set to be 150% and 2.5%, respectively (CBRC 2011). Then, in 2018, the CBIRC relaxed the requirements by allowing the regulatory minimums for the allowance-to-NPLs ratio and the allowance-to-total loans ratio for certain banks to be as low as 120% and 1.5%, respectively (CBRC 2018). The exact regulatory minimum for a particular bank mainly depends on the accuracy of loan classification, disposal of NPLs, and capital adequacy. The banking regulator issues risk warnings and rectification orders to banks in violation of the regulatory standard and may take other regulatory measures pursuant to China’s Banking Supervision Law (Article 23 ofCBRC 2011). 4.2. Regulatory Pressure and NPL Concealment Figure 4shows clustering of NPL transactions in the week before each quarter-end. Throughout our sample from 2014 to 2019, the average number of transactions in March, June, and September account for around half of all transactions in the first, second, and third quarters, respectively; the average number of transactions in December alone is two-thirds of all transactions in the fourth quarter. Altogether, transactions in the last month of each quarter account for double the number of transactions in other months. The quarter-end clustering of NPL transactions suggests that binding financial regulations drive NPL transactions as quarter ends are the points of time when reporting of financial conditions to the regulator is due. This pattern is consistent with extant evidence that regulation based on a non–continuously sampled point-in-time evaluation of bank risk introduces window dressing incentives (Paish 1939,Allen and Saunders 1992,Hoag 2016,Du et al. 2018,Cai et al. 2019). Figure 5shows the histogram of the allowance-to-NPLs ratio in the bank-year sample. The distribution exhibits bunching to the right of the 150% minimum. In untabulated analyses, we find no or less obvious bunching around the cutoffs of other regulatory ratios such as the allowance-to-total loans ratio, the capital ratio, or the loans-to-deposits ratio. In subsequent analyses, we focus on the 150% minimum allowance-to-NPLs ratio due to the regulatory cutoff. We directly test whether NPL regulation violations predict NPL concealment in a linear panel regression using a balanced bank-year sample.21We use the following specification: Table 5shows that banks in violation of the 150% minimum allowance-to-NPLs ratio are more likely to conduct NPL transactions. Column (1) shows the reported NPL ratio does not predict the likelihood of NPL transactions in the subsequent year. Column (2) shows banks in violation of the 150% minimum allowance-to-NPLs ratio are 25% more likely to conduct NPL transaction in the subsequent year, over half the unconditional probability of having an NPL transaction of around 40%. The differential predictability implies that the sheer size of NPLs does not linearly affect banks’ tendency to conceal NPLs; rather, the impact depends on the banks’ position in satisfying the regulatory minimum of the allowance-to-NPLs ratio. Banks’ incentives to conceal NPLs are strongest when the allowance they set aside for loan losses falls short of covering NPLs according to the regulatory minimum. To comply with the regulation, they can either set aside higher allowances at the expense of their bottom line, or they can off-load some NPLs to AMCs to lower the required allowance. Our estimate shows that banks in violation of the 150% minimum allowance-to-NPLs ratio actively use the NPL offloading opportunity to relax their regulatory constraint. Table 5.Determinants of NPL Transactions Table 5.Determinants of NPL Transactions Notes.This table examines the determinants of NPL transactions. Observations are at the bank-year level, wheretindexes a year and we drop bank subscripts.1⁢{𝑁𝑃𝐿𝑇𝑟𝑎𝑛𝑠𝑎𝑐𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛𝑡}1⁢{NPLTransactiont}is an indicator of whether a bank had any NPL transactions in a year. All regressions include controls for bank health and local economic conditions, including the lagged deposit growth, the lagged loan growth, the lagged reported GDP growth in the banks’ headquarter province, and the lagged growth in electricity usage in the banks’ headquarter province, as well as year and bank fixed effects. Thetstatistics testing the coefficients with zero are shown in parentheses, where standard errors are clustered by bank. ***, **, and *Statistical significance at the 1% level, 5% level, and 10% level, respectively. To shed further light on the regulatory drivers of NPL concealment, we augmentEquation (2)with an interaction term between regulatory violation and regulatory intensity. The extant literature on the Chinese banking sector motivates our measures of financial regulatory pressure across different regions.22First, as a measure of regulator strictness, we consider a regulator less than 52 years old, the median age across top regulators across all CBIRC offices, as a young regulator. Second, we consider a regulator as connected to the central government based on whether the regulator has working experience with the central CBIRC. Both young regulators and centrally connected regulators tend to be stricter due to career concerns. Additionally, we consider two sources of time-series variation corresponding to relaxation of financial regulation. Third, we consider years when bank reserve requirements are lowered. Fourth, we consider the period after 2018 when the CBIRC allowed the allowance-to-total loans ratio for certain banks to be as low as 120% if conditions on accuracy of loan classification, disposal of NPLs, and capital adequacy are met. By interacting regulatory violation with either measure of regulation relaxation, we allow for the impact of regulatory violation on NPL concealment to differ across time periods with different regulatory intensity. The direct effects of these time series variables are subsumed by the year fixed effects and therefore excluded from the estimation. Table 6reports the results. Columns (1) and (2) show that banks in violation of the NPL regulation that face stricter regulators are around 60%–140% more likely to conceal NPLs than those facing less strict regulators. Conversely, columns (3) and (4) show that when financial regulation is relaxed, banks in violation of the NPL ratio are 37%–66% less likely to conceal NPLs, although the 37% estimated differential impact of lower reserve requirements is not statistically significant at conventional levels. Overall, banks appear to conceal NPLs more when regulation is stricter and less when the regulation is relaxed. All regressions include controls for bank health and local economic conditions, including the lagged deposit growth, the lagged loan growth, the lagged reported GDP growth in the banks’ headquarter province, and the lagged growth in electricity usage in the banks’ headquarter province, as well as year and bank fixed effects. Time series variables are absorbed by year fixed effects. Violation is an indicator for violating the 150% minimum for allowances-to-NPLs ratio.tstatistics testing the coefficients with zero are shown in parentheses, where standard errors are clustered by bank. ***, **, and *Statistical significance at the 1% level, 5% level, and 10% level, respectively. 4.3. Regulatory Pressure and Transaction Financing Our data also include information on how each NPL transaction was financed. If the financing comes from the originating banks, we observe three possible exact sources of funds: a direct loan from the originating bank to the AMC, a financing agreement from the originating bank via a trust company—a nondepository banking institution that is regulated by the CBIRC—to the AMC, and a financing agreement from the originating bank via a securities company to the AMC. The funds provided by a given originating bank through either one of these categories occurs exactly at the time of the NPL transaction and is ring-fenced to purchase the NPL package from that bank. In all but nine NPL transactions, the financing agreement includes an appendix stating that the funds shall only be used to purchase the NPL of the issuing banks.23For a transaction whose source of funds does not fall into one of these three categories or is unavailable, its source of funds is recorded as “other means.” Anecdotally, executives at the local AMC suggest financing in the residual category still traces back to the originating banks but through more complicated channels. Thus, to be conservative, we do not treat this residual category as being funded by banks in our analyses. In July 2019, a rule was issued by the CBIRC to ban the direct lending of banks to local AMCs to finance NPL transactions. This regulatory ban reveals that the regulators are aware of some illicit concealment activity. The composition of the source of funds changed significantly after the rule. Panel A ofTable 7shows that before the restriction, more than two-thirds (68.5%) of transactions were financed via a direct loan; around a quarter of NPL transactions were financed through a bridge financing agreement via either a trust company or a securities company. Only 4% of transactions were financed through other means. After the July 2019 restriction banned direct loan financing, 88% of transactions were financed via a bridge financing agreement, and 12% through other means (Panel B). Table 7.Source of Funding for NPL Transactions Table 7.Source of Funding for NPL Transactions Notes.Summary statistics of the source of funds the AMC uses for each NPL transaction. Panel A shows the financing from before the July 2019 rule banning banks from lending directly to AMCs to finance NPL sales, and Panel B shows the financing after the rule. dec, decimal. All figures are rounded to either three significant digits or three decimal places, whichever is shorter. In Panels A and B, observations are at the NPL transaction level. We examine the relationship between regulatory violation and the financing of NPL transactions prior to the July 2019 rule change. For the originating banks who provide funds for the AMC to finance the NPL transactions, lending directly to the AMC to finance an NPL transaction is more detectable than indirect financing through a trust or securities company. We posit that banks with poorer health would be more likely to use direct loans than banks that could afford to arrange affiliated vehicles to finance the transaction and test this prediction using a regression of the form: Table 8shows that none of the four measures of NPL quality used inTable 3correlate with whether banks made a direct loan to the AMC to finance the transaction. Instead, we find that a bank with a one-standard-deviation lower capital ratio is 70%–85% and one that violated the NPL ratio is 55%–60% more likely to finance the NPL transaction with a direct loan. These results are economically large relative to the unconditional probability of 68.5%. Banks under more regulatory pressure to offload their NPLs are much more likely to make a direct loan to the AMC to expedite the purchase their NPLs. Future Bank Health and Credit Allocation To study NPL transactions and future bank violations, we consider a panel regression specification of the form: Table 9.NPL Transactions and Future Bank Health Table 9.NPL Transactions and Future Bank Health Notes.This table shows whether NPL transactions predict future bank health. Observations are at the bank-year level, wheretindexes a year and we drop bank subscripts. The dependent variable is an indicator of whether a bank violates the 150% minimum allowance-to-NPL ratio in the next year. All regressions include controls for bank health and local economic conditions, including the lagged deposit growth, the lagged reported GDP growth in the banks’ headquarter province, and the lagged growth in electricity usage in the banks’ headquarter province, as well as year and bank fixed effects. In both panels, thetstatistics testing the coefficients with zero are shown in parentheses, where standard errors are clustered by bank. ***, **, and *Statistical significance at the 1% level, 5% level, and 10% level, respectively. We also examine the effect of NPL concealment on banks’ credit allocation. The opportunity to transfer NPLs to AMCs allows banks to improve their balance sheet health and may potentially improve their credit allocation. However, the global proliferation of zombie lending, that is, banks’ continued extension of credit to unworthy distressed borrowers (Banerjee and Hofmann 2018,Altman et al. 2021) cast doubt on this view. To empirically test how NPL transactions affect credit allocation, we use detailed data on bank loans to listed firms from the Wind Financial Terminal, which are compiled from mandatory disclosures of listed firms. Therefore, we believe the data are accurate and complete for bank lending to this subset of listed firms that constitute a major part of banks’ commercial lending. We define zombie firms following bothAcharya et al. (2024)andAltman et al. (2021).24 We analyze how NPL concealment is associated with the allocation of credit inTable 10. The outcome variables are the total number of zombie and non-zombie listed firms that a bank lends to in a given year. We regress the lending measures against an indicator denoting the years after the bank offloads NPL for the first time to the AMC as observed in the data and the same set of control variables as before in a regression with bank fixed effects and year fixed effects. This empirical specification is akin to a two-way fixed effects (TWFE) difference-in-differences analysis. We also trace out the dynamic patterns by replacing the post indicator with a set of indicators for each year from two previous years to two post years of first offloading. We find that banks increase their zombie lending both statistically and economically. Regardless of the measure of zombie firms used, NPL-concealing banks increase their zombie borrowers by approximately 350% relative to the unconditional average number of 0.56 zombie borrowers in a year. The dynamic results in columns (2) and (4) show the increase in zombie lending only occurs in years after the NPL offloading. In columns (5) and (6), we do not find statistically significant results on NPL offloading with lending to non-zombie firms, although the point estimate is negative. This analysis shows that NPL offloading is followed by an increase of zombie lending and there is weak evidence of crowding out credit to healthy firms. article.5. Conclusion: In this paper, we used detailed transaction-level proprietary data from a leading local AMC and document that banks devise NPL transactions to conceal NPLs from regulators and financial markets. Because banks remain exposed to the NPLs that were sold, they represent hidden NPLs that should be recognized in calculating total NPLs. To study how potential realized losses may affect the financial system, we conduct a back-of-the-envelope stress test analysis assuming that our findings are representative of local AMCs and excluding national AMCs that are financial conglomerates with various other businesses. We find that hidden NPLs could amount to 2.81 to 4.62 trillion RMB in 2019 (between 117% and 192% higher than the official statistics), implying a total NPL ratio between 3.87% and 5.14% compared with the official reported NPL ratio of 1.86%. Details of the stress test are in the Online Appendix, Section A.7.25 Our back-of-the-envelope estimates are broadly consistent with concurrent analyses of China’s debt situation. The Bank for International Settlements Credit Statistics shows that China’s nonfinancial-debt-to-GDP ratio almost doubled from 139% at the end of 2008 to 259% at the end of 2019.26Risky debt increases to a high level against the backdrop of increasing leverage: Adopting a definition of a “loan potentially at risk” as a bank loan to a borrower that has an interest coverage ratio below one, the IMF points out that the share of loans potentially at risk tripled from 4% to more than 12% from 2010 to 2016. Meanwhile, using a different methodology,FitchRatings (2016)estimates that close to 20% of bank loans are de facto nonperforming. One reason that our estimates are lower than those from the IMF and Fitch is that the latter studies attempt to adjust for banks’ under-recognizing and evergreening de facto nonperforming loans. Our empirical evidence highlights a key friction that exists in many countries’ implementation of the bad bank model: an opaque market with special licensed entities interacting with heavily regulated banks can introduce perverse effects. Banks may find it more convenient to conceal NPLs by coordinating with the designated resolution specialists than to truly resolve them and recognize potential large losses. Furthermore, NPL-concealing banks extend more zombie credit, suggesting that an ineffective resolution exacerbates credit misallocation.
